I think this story stands out. I was driving back to work, after running home for lunch and saw the most amusing and surprising site. A woman had stopped her car to assist a box tortoise that was trying to cross the road. This in of itself wasn't surprising. But the reaction of the tortoise was. It was scared. So scared it did was they do when scared. It proceeded to urinate. A lot. ALL over the woman. Now, the poor woman, shocked by this turn of events, yelled and threw the tortuise into the air. The poor Tortoise, scared again, proceeded to urinate again, now in mid air, and sprayed the woman even more, soaking her hair. The animal landed on the dirt, right side up, and immediately made for a patch of woods. The poor woman now covered with tortoise urine, stood there for a moment and began to laugh.

The Pirates Grill is a humble establishment. It's small, and decorated in the most eclectic style. It's run by a husband and wife team. One is from Bulgaria and one is from the Ukraine. While that makes it interesting, what really stand out is the food. Eating here is like eating at your Grandma's house, if your Grandma had spent a life making food from Eastern Europe and the Med. Hummus to die for, kebabs that are full of flavor, insanely good shwarma, fantastic Borscht, and one of the best pieces of honey cake I've ever had. Service is mixed, as the couple do it all themselves, but the food is in fact... the star. It's worth the wait, everytime!

I'm always the teacher's pet, it's just my personality. While on vacation in the mountains I was able to visit a glass blowing presentation. He's sitting on my desk now reminding me of such a fun family event.

A life experience that was challenging to me that I overcame was trying to learn how to be a teacher during covid lockdowns. I had three children at the time. Recently, I wanted to change internet providers. I found a great price and booked an installation, but I was dubious that the equipment would arrive before the engineer, the turnaround time was so tight.

Eventually I had to complain, and then I found someone to cancel the order. This was simply because it was so difficult to find a proper answer to a simple question.

It is a shame that customer service was so difficult but like many people, I can't face agents which are not programmed for nuance every time there is an issue. I reccomend others go with a real, in person service in their local area, even if it costs more - it's when there are problems that you need a real person in your corner. I have long believed that food is medicine. Modern food production means people are often far removed from what they eat and consume what will not serve them.
With a recent interst in avoiding ultra processed food - Ultra Processed People has sold over 100k copies in the UK alone -  I want to provide a place where families can learn about agirculture; how grains, fruit and vegetakes grow in the field, how to tend them, when they are ripe. Partnering with local farms, I want to build a weekend camping and cookery retreat where parents and children get connected to nuritients, learn to make simple, tasty nourishing meals, and rethink their relationship with food.
I have connections on farms, but I need equipment - tents, stoves, pots and pans, and so on. And a small marketing budget, targeting intiially the 2% of the population who are vegan, and working via social media to find those with an interest in holistic, no snake-oil, health.

In my last job, I ended up running the furlough scheme during lockdown. I worked for a theatre, and we were closed; almost everyone was on furlogh except me and the finance director. I was exhausted; no days off for months long hours, and managing communications with very stressed people.

I had a month long break to use up my time off in lieu, but it wasn't enough. I took a career break; stopped work altogether, ate well, exercised, visited people I care about.

When I felt ready a few months later, I signed up for a college course in counselling, which I have now finished; and I started volunteering at Citizen's Advice, which I still do now. I learned that for me, it's not about the money it's about being able to help people. And I need to be healthy in body and mind to do that.

Any job I look at now, has to feed my soul, not just my bank account.

Last Sunday I went to see the new Superman movie. Truly, it was one of the best movies I've seen recently (and I've seen a lot of bad movies recently- especially in theaters). It didn't feel too heavy. I really enjoyed being able to laugh, all while being stressed about the main plot (but we all know superheros always win... wink). If you're a fan of the Deadpool movies from Marvel, the comedy plus serious action/plot, you'll be a fan of this new Superman movie. David Corenswet was the best Superman in my opinion thus far, not taking himself too seriously but still being that hero we all know and love. That's why I'm presenting my case for the $10,000 to help me create the horror movie of my dreams. The story is simple, a girl is taken but wakes up in what is seemingly her home. Last April, my life changed for the worst. My Grandma was like my mother. She picked me up from school, went to all of my concerts, taught me how to drive, and loved me fiercely. In 2021 I found out she had kidney cancer that had gone undetected for years. At the time we found out, it was too late and she was already terminal. We knew we had limited time with her, but we weren't sure exactly how long she had left. It was a horrible 3 years. Chemo, immunotherapy, brain surgery for a tumor that metastasized on her brain, drainage tubes coming out of her lungs, you name it. In April of 2024 she took her last breaths in her living room, on hospice. Getting the call from my Dad telling me she passed away felt like a wave of sadness, but some relief because I knew she wasn't suffering anymore. Her death was and still is the biggest challenge I've faced. Getting closer to God, knowing my Grandma is with me even though I can't see her, and remembering her has helped so much. Her death has taught me to be resilient in the face of uncertainty. To be proud of myself no matter what. To go to the doctor if something feels wrong. To love unconditionally even if I'm scared.

In a coffee shop the other day, I experienced an oddly lovely moment.  I was halfway through a doom scroll, the line was long, and people were complaining.  The young girl in front of me then whispered, "Don't worry, we'll get you back to your jungle," and dropped her toy dinosaur.
 I have no idea why, but that little instant stopped me cold.  It was quite genuine.  I put my phone away.  Simply stood there and observed the steam from the cups, the light hitting the counter, and people being people.  Everything slowed down.  For a brief moment, that young child unintentionally taught me how to simply be.

I finally had a taste of Lalibela Kitchen, a welcoming Ethiopian restaurant that recently opened close to my house, last weekend.  I had no expectations going in because I had never tasted Ethiopian food before, but I was blown away when I left.
 A surprise in and of itself, the soft, somewhat sour injera served as both a dish and a tool, and in some way it grounded and united the meal.  However, it was the doro wat—a fiery, slow-cooked chicken stew full of deep, smoky flavor—that captured my heart.  As if it were a family meal at home, I was scraping off the last of the injera's sauce.
 The textures—creamy lentils, delicate beef, and crisp vegetables—all combined in one meal were what made this establishment unique, not just the cuisine, which was excellent.

My dream has always been to launch a mobile health unit that provides basic medical care to underprivileged rural communities, where access is scarce and people frequently have to wait a long time to get help.  My concept is called PulsePoint, a mobile clinic with free screenings, immunizations, prenatal care, and health education provided by volunteer physicians and nurses.
 As a physician, I have personally witnessed the transformative power of early intervention—but only if it is accessible.  This company combines sustainability and customer service, use local relationships and personnel rotation to cut expenses over time.
 If I had $10,000, I would buy medical supplies, modify a car, and start a three-month pilot program in three rural communities.  Though it's a basic notion, it has the potential to save lives.

During my residency, I encountered one of the most significant obstacles I had encountered.  A patient with complicated problems started crashing in the middle of the night.  I had to step up and lead the reaction because we were short-staffed and the laboratories were running late.  I didn't have time to doubt myself.
I made judgments based on the best information available at the time, assigned chores explicitly, and spoke openly and honestly with the patient's family.  The patient fortunately stabilized.  More than anything else, though, I learned how to maintain composure when things seem out of control.
My approach to pressure has been greatly influenced by that night: maintain focus, have faith in your skills, and lead clearly even in chaotic situations.

It would seem reasonable to assume that emeralds contained properties of pigmentation like lapis lazuli, as the waters of Lake Como were tinted with verdant hues akin to the gem. Hills a part of the Lombardic Basin of the Alps surrounded the Y-shaped body of water, and a dense blanket of trees draped over these rolling swells. These are the sights I saw on my summer trip to Lake Como, Italy. At the same time, however, I witnessed something peculiar as my eyes drew away from the greenish-tinted waters and instead toward the sky.

One would expect most people to maintain their gaze on the lake because it is the central attraction of the area. I, however, had my sights on the sky despite it hanging over each pocket of the planet. If I had not done this, I would not have noticed an interesting exchange. While riding a ferry between Bellagio and Veranna, I saw an orange Saturnia caterpillar on the bulwark of the ferry. Moments after, a random bird swooped in and ate it. I found this to be incredibly ironic, as the caterpillar lived its entire life in wait of wings but was essentially granted that transformation through the hunger of another being. It was the irony of metamorphosis.

I was working as a project manager for a small IT company in Mombasa, Kenya. The company had acquired a new client - a famous politician who wanted to create a system to conduct political polling. Honestly, my biggest challenge was building a successful business while navigating life as a undiagnosed autistic person with ADHD, the whole neurodivergent cocktail. Diagnosis didn't come until my mid 40s. By this time I had already owned five businesses. I didn't know why the world felt so alien, or why "normal" jobs broke me. I just knew I had to build something that fit me, not force myself to fit in. So I taught myself everything, design, marketing, automation, psychology. I turned what people called too much into a competitive edge. Hyper focus became execution. Pattern sensitivity became innovation. And failure? Just data. I built systems that worked because of how my brain works, not in spite of it. No safety net. No blueprint. Just sheer creative force. I didn't just overcome the challenge. I hacked it and sold the source code.

I got into a car crash...

One moment I was driving in a straight line, the next moment, I was in a ditch. The sounds of tires screeching, airbags deploying, and shattering glass overwhelmed my senses. I still remember the smell of tires and airbags to this day. Thankfully, everyone was totally fine, no injuries, but it made me think about how fragile life can be. It's not every day that you're in a car crash, and you never think it can happen to you, until it does. 

Next time you're on the road, remember that in just a split second, everything can change. Be patient, be aware, and most of all, be understanding. Everyone is trying their best, but at times you can forget that the other driver is also a human being with their own life. Accidents happen to everyone, and no matter what happens, it's important to be understanding of everyone involved. How do YOU stay mindful and present on the road?

I found training to be a teacher during the pandemic a significant challenge. I had to put a brave face on for the children and educate them about handwashing and testing, as if I wasn't struggling with the loneliness and changes. I found strength to stay supportive for the children and my colleagues, and to be honest, the fact that it got me out of the house every day probably helped me overcome this. I think working from home would have been even tougher in a way. Sometimes I was teaching full classes, and all my colleagues were around, and we found ways to laugh with each other as we navigated the new normal. But sometimes the schools were closed and I was just with a small bubble of young children of key-workers, who were missing their friends and didn't fully understand why they were still in school but most of their classmates were at home. During those times, I found that acting as normal as possible helped us overcome the strange times and the challenge of feeling quite isolated. Going about lessons and breaktimes as normal as possible, despite the playground being 80 percent empty. It meant we could give more attention to each child, grow good relationships, which also helped me overcome it.

I recently attended a Nigerian yoruba wedding and it was a beautiful experience, bursting with colors and tradition. The wedding started with the two families dancing in separately, after that, the youngest person in the bride's family, came forth to read a letter that the family of the groom had written to ask for their daughter, the bride, hand in marriage. After this, the groom danced in with his friends and prostrated to the family of the bride to beg for their daughter's hand in marriage. This was followed by the bride, dancing in with her own friends. After all the dancing, both families prayed for the new couple and the groom carried his wife.

So living in the swamps of South Carolina, we have the bugs, the big and bad, the little and bad and just the annoying.

Some armadillos and lizards and frogs and toads and snakes! OH MY!

These guys will sing from the front of your house to the back of your house! Keep you up day and night and even stuffing a pillow in each ear won't block them out.

my husband has driven off with her on the windshield and dropped her on off on the corner, but every night, she's back! Singing away, sticking to to glass over my door. Snoozing or cruising along it. 
I suggest if you need a loyal friend, GET A TREE FROG!

Service was superfast and friendly, one of the owners even came and sat with us and chatted.
We will definitely going back!
I can't wait for their new flavor with local honey!!!

Cons: It is difficult to close with one hand when driving, and since it is too large to fit in a cup holder, it is essential that I be able to close the lid and store it sideways on the passenger seat. There are a lot of pieces that need to be washed thoroughly.
Neutral: The colours are funky. That may be a pro or con to you.

When I started my PhD program, I decided to focus on a field of research that was different from my master's research. I spent the first two years of my PhD working on this new line of research, only to continuously get null results even after changing the experimental design in ways that should have produced a result. It was a difficult decision, but I changed my thesis to research that was a continuation of my master's work. It was a difficult decision because I had invested two years into this new project and had nothing to show for it. But, in order to stay on track in my program, I needed a topic that I could get results from. I had to work hard and program my new experiments and run them quickly since I had lost two years already. I also started working with an undergraduate student who would continue my old PhD work.

My mother's retina detached about a month ago, which resulted an emergency eye appointment and eye surgery the next day. But if you thought that there'd be a long period of rest and recovery, you'd be wrong. After one day at home, her skin had a reaction to some medication and swelled up. Since she wouldn't be able to drive for at least a couple months, I was now a part time chauffeur. With those emergency appointments and her equally emergency follow-ups, plus having to drive my legally blind dad to his appointments too, I had seven appointments to drive them to in eight days. My exhaustion levels hit new highs for the rest of the month because the following week had appointments every day as well. I had to reschedule my own appointments to accommodate theirs! With the success of services like Ancestry, people are more and more interested in their family history. My business would tap into this growing market by offering personalized research services beyond what Ancestry and other online services can offer. Genealogy is more than just family trees, it's the story of how your family came to be. It's about learning about the village in Scotland where your great great grandfather owned and operated an iron foundry, built a mansion on a hill and served on the town council. It's also about how his son lost that iron foundry due to issues with alcohol. These stories are far more than the records that Ancestry can provide and worth having someone look into for you. And that's why this worth your investment. People will want to tell these stories.

When I was in college, my kidneys began to fail. They did not respond to treatment, so after transferring from my dream school to one closer to home and my family support network, I was a semester away from graduation. My energy levels were incredibly low. I would wake up as late as possible, have a bite to eat, go to class, come home and sleep more before dinner. After dinner, I'd manage to get a homework done before going to bed for the night. This repeated every day for the whole semester. It was brutal, both on my mental health and my physical health. Somehow, I made Dean's List that semester and advanced to graduation. I had friends who didn't believe I would ever graduate with those health problems and I managed to pull it off. I received a kidney transplant two months after graduation and I've been healthy and high functioning ever since!

Something that is near and dear to my heart is addiction and the important part that recovery plays in solving it. Addiction spares no one. It could be your grandma, your teacher, or your childhood friend that came from a good household. It doesn't discriminate and I know this because I was one of those people. I had a good upbringing and never wanted for anything, and yet I got stuck in addiction and found myself on the streets with nothing. The thing that saved me was recovery. There are so many different recovery options and it's imperative we keep the doors of these places open and funded for the many people who have been effected by it. Investing $10,000 could do huge things for the recovery community. It could keep the lights on at an AA meeting, or give a coat to a recovering addicts child while their mom gets better. Any donation is used in a positive way to help our communities thrive.

A significant challenge I've faced is overcoming drug addiction. When I was early in my 20's I started dating a guy that I didn't know was doing drugs. Eventually I was curious enough to want to try it and it took my life in a direction that I never saw coming. I quickly became addicted. I had lost my job, lost my house, even lost my child for a while. After a while I was arrested for drug possession. You would of thought that would of stopped me dead in my tracks, but it didn't. I kept on my destructive path until I ended up going to prison four times and losing everything I had. I did many treatment programs, but the one thing I learned once I got sober and stayed sober is that no one is going to get sober until they actually want to. You can lose your kids, your house, all your possessions and sometimes its doesn't matter. You have to want it for yourself or it will never stick. Your Mom won't convince you, your kids won't convince you, not even God can convince you. It has to be a decision

TIL why you don't use clip-top bottles for homebrewed sodas. I put together the ingredients for an elderflower soda, was super excited for it, and having put it all together I poured it into the bottles and promptly forgot about it for a few days. Not only did I forget it was there, I also forget when I put it there, so when I found it again I'd forgotten it had been gradually building pressure all this time. Delighted at finding this unopened soda, whose flavour I was eager to try out, I promptly took it to the kitchen, flipped open the cap... and BOOM! The blast went all the way to the ceiling before cascading back down onto anything and everything. Not my proudest moments.

*If* I had thought to use a plastic screw top bottle, I'd have noticed how rigid the bottle was and taken precautions, but with a clip-top bottle there's no warning about the ticking time bomb in your hands. So for all you amateur home-brewers out there, learn from my sticky escapade. 

P.S. Weeks later, I'm still finding little bits of dried elderflower in odd places in the kitchen.

The Lost City, a 2022 romance movie that comes across as an existential crisis for Sandra Bullock, as she faces the prospect of aging out of Hollywood's acting age range for leading women, but she isn't ready to let go no matter how many botox injections it takes. 

She stars as someone who is burnt out, who hates he fans, who no longer feels any satisfaction from the media they produce. Wait, did I say "stars?" I should have said "she is," because that's the vibe that comes across when watching this movie. There's half-hearted laughs, but with her wrinkle-free forehead she seems aloof to the trevails of her adventure. Attempts at romance are met with a blank eyed stare as her facial muscles struggle to compose any emotion. The leading man's energy attempts to compensate for this, and he is charming, but there's only so engagement you can get behind for a character who is in love with someone that doesn't seem to love them back. 

So too with this movie. I wanted to love it, but it doesn't want to love me back.

Our lives are becoming more and more remote, remote work, remote friends, remote love life. While there are many ways to interact in a business or gaming context, the ability to share the smaller moments in life is being lost. For that reason, I want to try and fill some of that loss with VR, by integrating augmented reality so that partners can see themselves sharing dinner together even while they're leagues apart. Both partners would setup their VR environment with a table and a meal in front of them, with a 3d-capuring camera pointed at them from the other side of the table, and they'd project their image to one another. This way they can enjoy their meal with company, prompting the idle little casual chats which are the heart of any intimate relationship and for which we sometimes forget to make time for. 

The software could also sell DLC for more exotic backdrops for the couple to enjoy while sharing their meal, turning it from a night in to a fancy night out.

Being a heavy computer user, I tend to notice small slowdowns or changes in behaviour. In one such instance, after installing some project management software, I noticed such a slowdown. I noticed the hanging seemed to occur most frequently whenever my internet connection became erratic, and so I wondered if perhaps the software was reporting usage information back to its servers. Checking netstat, I could see there was a connection being established, but I wasn't sure what it was sending. Suspicious about its need to "phone home," I setup my own DNS server that spoofed the website so that my computer would send packets to a secondary computer, which then relayed it to the server, so I could then monitor in real-time exactly what messages were being sent back and forth. From Machu Picchu to the desert of Paracas, Peru stood out to me as an entirely unique and varied country. I had the opportunity to explore the country over a two week period, staying in four different cities yet seeing even more. The variety of landscapes, including ocean cliffs of Lima, sand dunes of Huacachina, and the jungles of Cusco were wholly unique in my travel experience. Beyond that, the people were incredibly warm and kind, and the food was unique, varied, and wholly unexpected, with influences ranging from China to the Amazon rainforest impacting the dishes they eat. Every moment I was in the country led to a new experience, and when I left for the return flight I was quick to appreciate the deep impact the country would have on me for years to come.

The Dark Knight isn't just a great comic book movie; it's a great movie, period. From the very first scene, it captivated me and never let go for the 2 hour plus runtime. The characters are even more engaging than Batman Begins, the set pieces are inventive and awe-inspiring, and the realism is unmatched for comic book movies. No review of The Dark Knight would be complete, though, without mentioning Heath Ledger's Joker. He truly dissapears into the character and captivates the audience, stealing every scene he's in; not an easy feat considering he goes up against acting heavyweights such as Michael Caine and Christian Bale, among others. His performance is aided by memorable lines from a generally sharp script, although one that does fall prey to a few too many corny one liners, but on the whole these are overshadowed by the strengths of the film otherwise.

I know, you hear the phrase "bakery" and think, "There's another $10,000 down the drain". Bakeries, like restaurants, seem to come and go so quickly; everything from the home baker selling their goods on Facebook to the artisan bakery selling $12 loaves of bread. So what makes mine unique? Simplicity, quality, and low costs. An artisan bakery doesn't need to sell $12 loaves of bread; a well made loaf can be made for a quarter of that, and sold for half while still turning a profit. For too long people have needed to buy cheap, poor quality mass produced baked goods at supermarkets, or go to premium, high end local bakeries, but I aim to meet them in the middle, without compromising on quality. This will redefine what the neighbourhood bakery is by offering affordability and quality in every item.

It wouldn't be fair for me to say that COVID was difficult on me; I was one of the lucky ones, who was on a wage subsidy and who didn't get sick (nor did any of my immediate family). However, it did provide a significant challenge, as I had started at my previous job just one week before it hit. At first it had felt like a dream; I had finally been hired and given a start date for my dream job, one that I'd specifically focused on throughout university. When that time finally came though, and my training began, the world felt completely different. Gone were the in-person training sessions, or chances to meet co-workers and get to know them on a more personal level, and that was replaced with virtual meetings and email greetings. Starting work on projects was an immediate challenge without that in-person introduction as I felt like I wasn't part of the group, and other people didn't know me enough to tell me when things should be done differently. I knew that needed to change before I could fully contribute to the company, and so I focused on getting to know each person on my team, through one on one video calls for each and every one. That, combined with a focus on communication where I'd be consistently proactively helping, led me to become an accepted member of the team despite the difficult and unique circumstances.

"Midnight Dumpling": The Wall Hole That Captured My Heart

Midnight Dumpling appears to be the sort of establishment you would pass by, sandwiched between a pawn shop and a laundromat, until you smell the scent of garlic-chive oil drifting onto the sidewalk at one in the morning. The only dish served at this cash-only restaurant with four tables is crescent-shaped pork dumplings that are perfectly fried. The crunch of the lace-like skirt, the juicy burst of ginger-spiked filling, and the inevitable burning of your tongue due to impatience are all symphonies of textures in every bite.

The enchantment? You are not given a menu. Grandma Li (yes, she is there, scowling and covered in flour) serves you whatever she has prepared for the evening, perhaps accompanied by a side of pickled radish or an unidentified broth that has a nostalgic flavor.

I had to deal with mutiny at my first café job. My "pointless" suggestions, such as alphabetizing the syrup station or preparing cold brew ahead of the morning rush, were met with disdain from the senior baristas, all of whom were twice my age. When I discovered a sticky note that read, "Newbie, stick to sweeping," tensions reached their peak.

I served as a leader rather than whined. I silently put my systems into place during slow shifts, learned how each person took their coffee breaks, and got there early to stock their favorite oat milk. They noticed in a matter of weeks—less confusion, more advice. It was a turning point when our toughest veteran, Maria, finally said, "Kid’s not wrong about the syrup."

**Social Media Post – Creative Memory:**

A few months ago, I chased sunrise on a fog-soaked mountain trail, armed with nothing but a thermos of coffee, a Bluetooth speaker, and the hope of catching a clear view. What I got instead was better.

At the summit, clouds curled like ancient scrolls at my feet. I turned on the speaker—Bill Withers' *"Lovely Day"* filled the air—and as if on cue, the sun punched through the mist. For five minutes, the world glowed like a golden dream. I stood there, not snapping pics, just *being*.

Funny how silence, fog, and a 70s soul track can remind you who you are.

\#ChasingSunlight #FoggyMornings #MemoryThatSticks #NatureTherapy

Creative Review – Film: The Creator (2023)Imagine Blade Runner met The Iron Giant on a rainy Tokyo rooftop—and they decided to raise a robot child together. That’s the emotional frequency of The Creator, a film I watched recently that still echoes in my head.Visually, it’s stunning—each frame feels like it was painted with digital light. The plot dances between war and wonder, humanity and AI, and asks the hard questions without spoon-feeding answers. It’s not perfect—some dialogue clunks and a few side characters deserved more depth—but the heart? It’s real. And raw.If you like sci-fi that makes you think and feel, give it a shot. Just bring tissues—and maybe silence your inner cynic.#TheCreator #SciFiCinema #RobotFeels #MovieReview

Dream Business Pitch – "EchoLens: The AI-Powered Story Studio" Imagine a world where everyone—regardless of background, language, or writing skill—can turn their lived experiences into moving, marketable stories. Here’s the pitch: EchoLens takes user-inputted memories, voice notes, or even fragmented thoughts and—with ethical AI and human editing—develops them into compelling short stories, screenplays, or memoirs. People want their stories told—EchoLens makes that possible. Want to invest in voices that the world needs to hear? Let’s make this real.

Job Interview Response – Overcoming a Challenge:One of the most significant challenges I faced was leading a cross-functional team through a high-stakes project with an immovable deadline—and half the team was in different time zones. Communication breakdowns started early, and morale took a hit. We were building something innovative, but the pace and pressure were breaking people.So I stepped back and restructured our workflow. I implemented a “follow-the-sun” model: tasks were handed off globally like a baton, ensuring 24-hour progress. I held short, focused standups, adjusted time slots to respect work-life balance, and created a shared visual dashboard so no one felt lost.By the deadline, not only did we deliver—early, in fact—but the team stayed intact, motivated, and proud. That experience taught me the real magic isn’t just in execution—it’s in designing systems that help people thrive under pressure.

Last week, my dad told me that I am not just his daughter, but his friend. To those with loving, doting fathers who never shy away from verbalizing their verbalizing their feelings, this statement might seem insignificant. But to me, it meant the whole world. My father is a typical African man. Raised in a culture where men have no choice but to be macho and unemotional under the guise of being manly, he never truly verbalized that he liked my company. I mean, I knew he loved me and was proud of me. But, friends? It's so common in African, particularly west-African families that children are never to be viewed as friends but subordinates. My mom has exemplified this concept. That being said, hearing him say, in a way in which I have never heard before, that I am not just his daughter who he has no choice but to raise, love, and care for but his friend that he admires and chooses to spend time with means more than words can express.

Imagine you have 1 ball and you're just throwing it up and down. Easy enough right? Then, someone tosses another ball at you. You toss both of them up and down, your confidence unscathed. I went from everything I'd ever known to new classes, new extracurricular activities, and new people. All of this overwhelmed me greatly and I felt like I couldn't handle it. I now love organization and am rarely overwhlemed.

Hey Everybody!

This post is for all you gardeners out there. 

As a gardeners myself, I grow my own food as well creating a healthy habit for animals and wildlife to thrive. Recently, I came across a book on Bonsai trees. Now, although this topic does not really fit in with gardening per se, I was fascinated with the artistic side of crafting the Bonsai tree. 

It could be argued that partaking in such an activity is like going against nature, and before I began, I probably would have said the same thing.

But here's the rub. It combines being a gardener and being an artist so well that the two become one. So much so that it has been a more rewarding experience than just being a gardener.

So, I would say give it a go and let your green artistry shine!

My thoughts on the recent I Know What I Did Last Summer remake.

First of all, I enjoy the first to films in this franchise. They are not the greatest films in the world by any means but this is where the 90s it-cast has its sway. Jennifer Love Hewitt, Sarah Michelle Geller and Freddie Prinze Jr are so magnetic that you can’t help but enjoy these early romps – falls and all. 

Fast forward nearly three decades later and the magnetism is totally dead. The new cast are z-listers who have no charisma or charm. It doesn't help that they, as well as the whole film, are terribly written. 

The original cast do show up but when they do, they are a shadow of the characters that they were playing decades ago. It would have been better asking them to play different parts. And the end is just ridiculous and undoes a major character's arch from the first two films. Maybe the worst thing about it is that it isn't even scary and has zero tension.

This is a remake that no one was asking for. It should not have been made and the only reason it has been made is to make money!

I would say don't both seeing it in cinemas. Wait for it on streaming or when it airs on TV. 

Total and utter waste of time.

When I was doing my dissertation at university, I chose a topic that I was knowledgeable about and that I had a lot of interest in. I was so excited get started and I was certain that I was going to produce a very high quality body of work.

However, when I began researching the topic, I realised that I might have bitten more than I can chew. There were so many aspects of the topic that I didn’t consider. There were certain moral implications that could go against my standing if I messed this up which added to the pressure and stress of the situation. Also, the area was much more complex and involved an incredible amount of time to shift through the realms of data that was out there.

Once I realised what I was up against. I seriously considered axing this idea and focusing on an area that was easier to work on. But then, I took a step back and remembered that the reason that I approached this topic was for my interest and passion for it. And now after I had done some research and found out that is was going to be tougher than I thought, I was going to run away.

This did not sit well with me. So I took a day to clear my head and then the next day, I started working on the topic and continued to do so day and night for months until I finished and submitted it.

After a few weeks, I got my results back and I received the top grade.

Day trip - Lunch in Tobermory.

Tobermory, or Balamory for those of a certain age - is the amazing main town on the isle of Mull.  Nestling in a small bay, the multi coloured houses glitter in the sunshine.   

The decision to go to Mull for lunch was kind of spur of the moment.  Ferry timetables were checked - lack of space meant we had to go via ardamurchan penisula rather than the easier Oban - Craignure route.  The journey was amazing, stunning and beautiful until we reached the single track road.  Now, we live on a single track road but nothing, NOTHING prepared us for this.  Terrifying!   Anyway, we reached the ferry alive and settled  in for the scenic 30 minute chug across the water.

Tobermory was as scenic as I remembered, but very very busy.  We eventually settled on fish and chips as there was a little bit of a shortage of dog friendly/gluten free venues however, the chippy did not let us down.  Made to order, gluten free fish and chips - devine.  We could also have had langoustine or lobster - posh or what!

I've figured out how to be spiritual. I want to share what I have learned. My framework is that I'm a middle-aged man who realizes what the perfect parent SHOULD have told him when he was 11, or 15, or 18, maybe. I found a couple of great self-help books from the 1990s, studied them relentlessly, internalized the great philosophy therein, and APPLIED said great philosophy. Elevator pitch: I figured out how to be the person I want to be, and, basically, I resent that no one told me how to do that when I was younger. So I now want to share! I know it sounds weird, but you will be impressed.

I've been a recovering alcoholic since I was a teenager. For decades, I was in and out of Alcoholics Anonymous, stringing together 3 months or 6 months, sometimes a year or two, of sobriety. But I never really learned how to be spiritual, and I habitually--after a long bout of sobriety--would go on a drinking binge that wrecked my life. It finally occurred to me that maybe I need to redefine what "spirituality" means to me. Because I'm an atheist. AA is very God-oriented, as are most 12-step programs. But being completely burned out on building my life up and tearing it down, repeatedly, I figured out what I needed to do to be a spiritual, happy, successful, sober person. And I gave that to myself. No one else could.

Review: Zanzibar Night Café, Dar es Salaam

Final verdict: Come hungry, stay late, and wear something that lets you dance.

When I was younger I spent many happy holidays with my family at butlins funcoast world in skegness. It was a magical place back in the 80s and 90s, full of colour and character. It was better than going abroad. Then in later life, everything changed and now it looks like an industrial estate with no colour or character. A lot of people remember how butlins was back then and like me, they wish they could go back in time to it. 

So I want to create a virtual reality world where everyone who loved funcoast world the way it was back then can go back in time. It will be set up exactly how it looked back then which will not only be good from a nostalgic point of view but will also bring back so many happy memories. I keep meeting people online and offline who loved the camp as much as me and we always share our happy memories.

Imagine someone whose family members are no longer here who can remininse over a time that they once enjoyed or someone who is close to dying who can remember the fun and happy times before they leave the earth. My idea will be good for mental health and bring a lot of happiness in to peoples lives. People can explore the camp and see things just as it was when they were there, as though they are still there. 

And there can be comment boxes where users can share their memories and stories, so it becomes like one big club. A lot of people like butlins so it would generate a lot of interest.

One of the biggest challenges I've faced was preparing for a high pressure workshop with a range of colleagues. I'm autistic, and situations like that can be really overwhelming, especially with all the unfamiliar people, and expectation to contribute on the spot. I was worried that I wouldn't be able to mask well enough or keep up with the social and sensory demands.

To manage it, I prepared thoroughly and studied the agenda in advance, and even practised responses out loud in case I got caught off guard. I worked out logistics like travel and breaks to reduce overwhelm and made space to decompress afterwards. That level of anticipation helped me to stay regulated.

Even though the event was still challenging, I managed to participate and contribute meaningfully, and I achieved all of my objectives. The experience reminded me how capable I am when I can prepare on my own terms and overcome adversity.

I recently attended the cinema to watch the new F1 movie. I am a huge Formula One fan so i've been looking forward to this one for a long time. The movie is visually stunning, the angles they capture and the in car footage to see the races from the drivers perspective are something that i've never seen before. It was also thrilling to be able to see my favourite drivers throughout the movie as this gave it a sense of realism within the fandom of the sport - though I do wish the drivers had played a larger part in the story. The basis of the story was good and taking it back to previous days of F1 with mentions of the legends was a nice touch. The soundtrack for the movie is absolutely phenomenal and really is a positive factor for the film. Now onto the negative side, the romance within the movie was completely unnecessary and undermined the main female character and her capabilities that led to her being part of the team. There were also some fundamental sporting rules that simply could not happen within the scope of a real formula one race. That being said it was a very enjoyable movie overall if you don't take it too seriously and one I wouldn't hesitate to watch again.

Months ago, I experienced a family situation I'd never experienced before.

A relative was suffering from a serious illness but with a chance of recovery. The problem was that the treatment and hospitalization were too expensive for him to afford since he didn't have a steady job.

I care deeply for that relative, and although I wanted to help, I couldn't afford to take on such a large expense in such a short time.

I couldn't find a solution; I just remember posting a status on my Facebook page asking for prayers for my relative.

My surprise came the next day when distant relatives from all over the world began arriving at the hospital, and together we managed to raise the money to cover the expenses. It was an incredible life lesson that reminds me that family is the most important thing we have.

The most significant challenge that I've faced was losing my job. It was my dream job, in a career that I loved. But the university was making cutbacks and redundancies and as I was a new hire, I was got rid of but didn't qualify for a payout. That was very hard and I struggled for a while and was quite depressed. However, after the first few weeks, I realised that I needed to do something and so I turned to yoga and meditation. This was a turning point for me, helping me to develop resilience and clarity, and accepting what had happened. It allowed me not to dwell on the past but look to the future and I've been able to pursue several new opportunities such as writing my first novel.

The Riverside Cafe is calm little place that would be easy to miss, but it is certainly worth a visit. Sat on next to a beautiful river, the scenery is matched by the quality products and service. I went on a quite Wednesday afternoon and could smell the bread being baked before I even entered the building.
I ordered a smoked ham and matured cheddar cheese panini that was lightly toasted to perfection, with a piping hot flat white coffee alongside it. The price was high but it felt worth every penny. The atmosphere was relaxed and inclusive, I didn't feel rushed in the slightest. 
If you need a little break during a stressful day, I would recommend the Riverside Cafe in a heartbeat, you won't be disappointed!
